title: He (Kaiming) Initialization
concept: He initialization is a layer-aware random start built for ReLU-style activations, helping the surviving signals stay strong enough even though many ReLU outputs become zero.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Xavier initialization is most often matched with which activation family?
exercises.explain: Exactly. Different activation families often want different initialization strategies.

cards.title: Half the hallway lights are off
cards.body: Picture a long hallway where many light switches are off, so only part of the light makes it to the far end. ReLU networks behave a bit like that: many activations become zero, so the remaining signal needs a good starting scale to stay useful.

cards.title: What He initialization is for
cards.body: He initialization is a layer-aware random initialization designed for ReLU-family activations. Because ReLU often shuts off many neurons to zero, He uses a scale that helps the active signals stay strong enough as they pass through deep layers.

cards.title: Speaking up when half the mics mute
cards.body: Imagine a panel discussion where half the microphones cut out at random moments. The speakers whose mics still work need enough volume to keep the conversation alive. He initialization plays a similar role for ReLU networks: it starts the surviving paths at a healthier strength.

cards.title: Deep ReLU stacks
cards.body: Suppose you build a deep image model with ReLU after every hidden layer. A generic small random start may leave later activations weak because so many ReLU outputs become zero. He initialization is a common fix because it is tuned to that ReLU behavior, giving the active neurons a better chance to keep useful signal flow.

cards.title: He helps, but it is not a magic shield
cards.body: A common myth is that He initialization makes any ReLU network impossible to train badly. Not true. It is a smarter starting point, not a guarantee. Learning rate, architecture, normalization, and data quality still matter.

cards.title: Generic start vs. ReLU-aware start
cards.body: He initialization matches the fact that many ReLU activations get cut to zero.

left.text:
ReLU zeros out many paths
Remaining signals can weaken too much
Deep layers may struggle

right.text:
Scale is chosen with ReLU in mind
Active signals stay healthier
Strong default for ReLU-family nets

exercises.prompt: He initialization is most commonly used with which activations?
exercises.explain: Correct. He initialization was designed specifically with ReLU-style behavior in mind.

exercises.prompt: True or false: He initialization is motivated by the fact that many ReLU activations become zero, so the remaining signal needs an appropriate scale.
exercises.explain: True. That is the key intuition behind why He differs from Xavier.

exercises.sentence: He initialization is a common default for ___ networks.
exercises.explain: Yes. When hidden layers use ReLU-family activations, He is a standard starting choice.

exercises.prompt: Which setup best matches He initialization?

options.text: A deep CNN with ReLU layers starts with a ReLU-aware random scale to keep active signals healthy.
A network with ReLU sets every hidden weight to zero so neurons stay identical.
A Tanh network uses He because He is always best for every activation.

exercises.explain: Exactly. He is the activation-aware choice that fits ReLU-family layers.

exercises.prompt: A deep vision model uses ReLU in every hidden layer. With a generic random start, later layers look weak because many activations are being zeroed out. What initialization change is the most sensible?

options.text: Switch to He initialization, because it is designed to keep ReLU networks' surviving signals at a healthier scale.
Switch to all-zero weights, because ReLU layers prefer perfect symmetry.
Use huge random weights so every activation becomes large no matter what.
Remove initialization entirely and let the network invent starting weights during evaluation.

exercises.explain: Right. He initialization is the standard fix because it accounts for the way ReLU shuts off many activations while trying to preserve useful signal strength.
